Q1: I kept things pretty simple with my study routine. I'd usually set aside time on weekends to watch the lecture videos and go through the visual materials since that's how I learn best. During weekdays, I'd squeeze in maybe an hour or two when I could, but honestly, I didn't have a super rigid schedule. I just made sure I completed what needed to be done, even if my attendance wasn't great.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines. I'm not someone who studies the same hours every day—I'd ramp up when assignments were due and ease off when things were quieter. With 15 online courses to manage, I had to be strategic about where I put my energy. Some weeks I'd barely touch certain courses, then catch up intensively before something was due.
Q3: Usually it was an upcoming deadline or a notification about new materials that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd just check to see if there were any announcements or if feedback had been posted. I wasn't someone who logged in daily just to browse—I went in with a purpose, got what I needed, and logged out.
Q4: It became routine mainly around assignment submission times and when new video content dropped. I'd log in, download or watch what I needed, then work offline. After a few weeks, I knew the rhythm of when things would be posted, so I'd check in accordingly. It wasn't like a daily habit, more like a weekly checkpoint.
Q5: The video lectures were honestly the most useful for me because I'm a visual learner. I'd watch them, sometimes pause to take screenshots or notes, and really absorb the material that way. The discussion boards were okay too—I posted when required and occasionally found other students' questions helpful for clarifying things I'd missed.
Q6: I often skipped the reading-heavy PDFs or text-based resources because they just don't stick in my brain the same way. If there was a video alternative, I'd always choose that. Some of the supplementary materials felt optional, so with limited time and resources, I prioritized what directly impacted my grades.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to assignments or exams. If I could see how it would help me perform better, I'd engage with it fully. Visual content, practice problems, and anything with clear relevance to assessments—those were my priorities. Vague or overly theoretical stuff got pushed aside.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—like nice-to-haves rather than must-dos. With my course load and limited resources, I couldn't afford to spend time on things that wouldn't directly impact my grade. Required activities got my full attention because I knew they mattered. Optional ones I'd only touch if I had extra time, which was rare.
Q9: Oh absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download videos and watch them later when my internet was spotty or when I wanted to save data. I'd take handwritten notes, make visual diagrams, and study from those. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS entirely.
Q10: Most of my exam prep wouldn't show up in the LMS at all. I'd review my notes, redraw diagrams, and quiz myself using materials I'd already downloaded. Sometimes I'd watch a video once online, then replay my downloaded version multiple times. The LMS would only show that single initial view.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access a video or document once to download it, then study from it offline for hours or even days. With my internet situation and limited resources, it made more sense to grab everything in one session and then work independently.
Q12: There were definitely moments when engagement got harder, usually when multiple courses had deadlines at the same time. I'd feel stretched thin and have to make tough choices about where to focus. My low attendance reflects that—I couldn't always keep up with live sessions when other pressures mounted. The CBT session actually helped me process some of that stress.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or overwhelmed with my 15 courses, I'd do the bare minimum on the LMS—just enough to stay on track. Confusion sometimes made me avoid certain materials temporarily until I could figure things out. Competing responsibilities meant I had to be very selective about my time online.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was elsewhere. I'd be tired or distracted, and even though I was technically accessing content, nothing was really sinking in. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than actual learning.
Q15: I'd sometimes postpone discussion posts or peer interactions because they felt less urgent than assignments. Even though I knew participation mattered somewhat, I'd prioritize things with clearer grade impacts. With low energy and motivation some weeks, the social aspects of the course were easiest to delay.
Q16: Deadlines basically dictated my LMS usage. I'd log in more frequently as due dates approached, watch videos I'd been putting off, and submit work right before cutoffs. My engagement spiked around deadlines and dropped off between them. It wasn't ideal, but it worked for managing everything.
Q17: Feedback affected me in surprising ways actually. Good feedback motivated me to maintain my approach, while critical feedback made me revisit materials I thought I understood. I'd sometimes log back in specifically to review content related to feedback I'd received. It helped guide where I needed to focus.
Q18: The course structure with clear deadlines and organized modules helped me plan my sporadic engagement. Knowing exactly what was due and when allowed me to work around my other commitments. The visual materials and video-heavy format suited my learning style well, which made participation feel more natural.
Q19: Having video content made engagement much easier for me as a visual learner. What made it harder was when there were too many text-heavy readings or when the workload across my courses overlapped badly. The discussion requirements sometimes felt forced, which made them harder to engage with authentically.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for understanding without finding it. They're clicking through materials, rewatching videos, trying to make sense of things but struggling to produce quality work. Or they might be dealing with personal issues that make it hard to translate activity into output. High activity doesn't always mean effective learning.
Q21: That actually describes me pretty well. My attendance was really low and I wasn't constantly on the LMS, but I still got a distinction. I studied efficiently offline, focused on what mattered for exams, and made the most of the time I did spend on materials. Quality over quantity, I guess.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend. But they miss the depth of engagement. They can't see me rewatching downloaded videos, making diagrams, or having those 'aha' moments while studying my notes. The numbers don't capture actual understanding.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious, when I want to figure something out rather than just check a box. For me, engagement happens in my head and my notes, not necessarily in how many times I log into a system.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I studied offline, the visual notes I created, or the genuine understanding I developed away from the screen. It wouldn't capture the mental effort I put into exam prep or how I connected concepts across different courses. My best learning happened invisibly.
